coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of surface molecularly imprinted poly-o-phenylenediamine/TiO2/carbon nanodots with a highly enhanced selective photocatalytic degradation of pendimethalin herbicide under visible light
coredata.dc:creator: Shahnazi, Azita
Nabid, Mohammad Reza
Sedghi, Roya
coredata.dc:description:
A molecularly imprinted polymer layer coated on TiO2/carbon nanodots (TiO2/CNDs/MIP) as a novel nanocomposite was successfully synthesized by means of the surface molecularly imprinted technique using pendimethalin (PM) herbicide as the template molecule. O-phenylenediamine was used both as the functional monomer for MIP and as the precursor of carbon nanodots. The structure and properties of the nanoparticles and nanocomposites were characterized by FE-SEM, EDX, TEM, DLS, XRD, XPS, PL, FT-IR, BET, and UV–vis DRS. The photocatalytic activity, adsorption capacity, and adsorption selectivity of TiO2/CNDs/MIP and TiO2/CNDs/NIP were investigated. The results indicated that TiO2/CNDs/MIP nanocomposite exhibited the high adsorption capacity of 86.1 mg/g in 30 min and an excellent selectivity toward PM compared to other structural analogues due to the imprinted cavities and specific recognition sites on the surface of MIP. In addition, the TiO2/CNDs/MIP nanocomposite revealed the enhanced photodegradation efficiency (about 95%) of PM compared with TiO2, TiO2/CNDs, and TiO2/CNDs/NIP, as a result of its large adsorption capacity to template molecules and its narrow band gap energy under visible light. Using charge carrier scavengers, it was revealed that the O2
− radicals were the main surface species for the photodegradation of PM. Moreover, TiO2/CNDs/MIP nanocomposite exhibited reusability and high stability.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of surface molecularly imprinted poly-o-phenylenediamine/TiO2/carbon nanodots with a highly enhanced selective photocatalytic degradation of pendimethalin herbicide under visible light

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Polymer and Materials Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry and Petroleum Sciences, Shahid Beheshti University, GC, 1983969411 Tehran, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Polymer and Materials Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry and Petroleum Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Polymer and Materials Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry and Petroleum Sciences, Shahid Beheshti University, GC, 1983969411 Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
A molecularly imprinted polymer layer coated on TiO2/carbon nanodots (TiO2/CNDs/MIP) as a novel nanocomposite was successfully synthesized by means of the surface molecularly imprinted technique using pendimethalin (PM) herbicide as the template molecule. O-phenylenediamine was used both as the functional monomer for MIP and as the precursor of carbon nanodots. The structure and properties of the nanoparticles and nanocomposites were characterized by FE-SEM, EDX, TEM, DLS, XRD, XPS, PL, FT-IR, BET, and UV–vis DRS. The photocatalytic activity, adsorption capacity, and adsorption selectivity of TiO2/CNDs/MIP and TiO2/CNDs/NIP were investigated. The results indicated that TiO2/CNDs/MIP nanocomposite exhibited the high adsorption capacity of 86.1 mg/g in 30 min and an excellent selectivity toward PM compared to other structural analogues due to the imprinted cavities and specific recognition sites on the surface of MIP. In addition, the TiO2/CNDs/MIP nanocomposite revealed the enhanced photodegradation efficiency (about 95%) of PM compared with TiO2, TiO2/CNDs, and TiO2/CNDs/NIP, as a result of its large adsorption capacity to template molecules and its narrow band gap energy under visible light. Using charge carrier scavengers, it was revealed that the O2
− radicals were the main surface species for the photodegradation of PM. Moreover, TiO2/CNDs/MIP nanocomposite exhibited reusability and high stability.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, intense agricultural practices have improved grain yields. However, the excessive use of agrochemicals involved in intense agricultural practices has led to herbicide accumulation in the soil, groundwater, and drinking water [1]. Dinitroaniline herbicides are a class of nitroaromatic compounds and include pendimethalin, trifluralin, dinitamine, nitralin, etc. Dinitroanilines usually enter the soil during agricultural applications and can also enter surface waters and sediments through surface runoff, field drainage, groundwater discharge, and spray drift [2]. Pendimethalin is a kind of dinitroaniline herbicide and is extensively used for various crops to control grassy weed species and broadleaf weeds and also to protect different crops such as cereals (barley, rye, triticale, and wheat), potatoes, legumes, corns, soybeans, vegetables, rice, fruits, and nuts besides ornamental plants and lawns. Pendimethalin hinders cell division and cell wall formation of harmful weeds and separates their chromosome [3]. The removal of herbicides is a challenging task in controlling environmental pollution. Recently, some methods including biological treatments [4], electrochemical oxidation [5], adsorption [6], combination of ozonation and photocatalysis [7], and photocatalysis [8–11] have been employed to remove herbicides from wastewater. Since a great number of herbicides are recalcitrant pollutants, they must therefore be destroyed and photocatalysis which can bring about a complete destruction or mineralization of the pollutant is well suited for this task [8]. Semiconductor metal oxide photocatalysis has been reported as an impressive green technology for solving environmental pollution problems [12]. Titanium dioxide, one of the most attractive semiconductor photocatalysts, has been widely studied due to its advantages such as chemical stability, high compatibility, long durability, unique optical characteristics, nontoxicity, low cost, and electronic properties [13]. Nonetheless, its application is limited because of its photocatalytic activity in the UV region (λ < 400 nm), its large band gap (~3.2 eV) [14], and its high charge carrier recombination rate. Moreover, TiO2 has some drawbacks such as the aggregation of nanoparticles due to the instability of the nanosized particles [15,16], its difficulty in distributing particles uniformly, its difficulty in recovering the nanosized particles, and its low adsorption of organic pollutants (especially hydrophobic organic pollutants) [17,18]. To overcome these limitations, the following countermeasures have been adopted: doping of metals (Cu, Co, Ni, Cr, Mn, Fe, Ru, Au, Ag, Pt) [19] and nonmetals (C, N, S, B, P, F, I) [20], co-doping technique [21], coupling with other semiconductors [22], surface organic modification by dye sensitization and organic coating [23], doping with carbon-based nanoparticles [13,24,25], stabilization by surface modification and support structures [26], immobilization on support structures [27], and magnetic separation [28]. Carbon doping (especially with nanostructured carbons such as nanotubes, graphene, and CNDs (carbon nanodots)) has been demonstrated as an effective way for the utilization of the visible light and further enhancing the photocatalytic activity of TiO2 [29]. When CNDs are combined with TiO2 nanoparticles, the catalytic activity of TiO2 is improved to different degrees depending on the intrinsic nature of electron transportation and conducting ability of CNDs. In addition to this, CNDs have a large number of functional groups such as –COOH, –OH, and -NH2 which can enhance the adsorption of organic pollutants [30]. Recently, coating TiO2 with an organic layer of molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) has also been employed to enhance its photocatalytic degradation efficiency through the MIPs' ability of recognition and specific adsorption of pollutants [31–35]. Molecular imprinting is a useful technique for the synthesis of polymeric materials including highly specific recognition sites for template molecules and shows the ability of selectively rebinding to template molecules from a matrix [36]. The recognition sites can be generated by the copolymerization of the functional monomers and cross-linkers in the presence of template molecules. During the polymerization process, functional monomers are arranged around the template molecules through either covalent or noncovalent interactions. Cross-linking reagents fix the functional groups in defined positions in the polymer matrix. Binding cavities are created with the same shape and size of the template after the removal of the template by chemical cleavage or solvent extraction [37]. However, molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) prepared by conventional techniques have some drawbacks such as incomplete template removal, low-affinity binding, high diffusion barrier, and low-rate mass transfer. In order to overcome these drawbacks, surface imprinting (2D imprinting) has been used as a new strategy for creating the recognition sites on the surface of suitable supports which could be more effective for eluting and rebinding the target molecules. Thus, 2D imprinting technique provides better site accessibility and can exploit the properties of the support material [38–40].

Up to now, there has been no report regarding the preparation of molecularly imprinted polymer coated TiO2/CNDs nanocomposite (TiO2/CNDs /MIP) by surface molecular imprinting and its application in selectivity photocatalytic degradation of herbicides. In the present work, carbon nanodots (CNDs) were prepared from o-phenylenediamine (OPDA) through the hydrothermal method. Moreover, the doping of CNDs was carried out by their assembly on TiO2 nanoparticle surfaces to shift the absorption edge from UV into visible light. In addition, OPDA and pendimethalin (PM) were chosen as the functional monomer and template molecule, respectively, for creating the MIP layer coated on TiO2/CNDs. The two -NH2 groups of OPDA not only are hydrophilic but also can interact with the functional groups (-NO2 and -NH) of the template molecules (PM) to make a precursor for the imprinting of the template molecules during the polymerization process [33]. Moreover, a polymer with a polyaniline-like structure produced from OPDA is photochemically stable and favorable to the photocatalytic efficiency under visible light. In addition, the excess OPDA failing to make a precursor with the template molecules can act as a cross-linker, enhancing the shape selectivity of the MIP layer [41]. The photocatalytic activity and the adsorption kinetics of the resulting TiO2/CNDs/MIP nanocomposite toward PM were studied. Furthermore, the selectivity of the adsorption and photodegradation processes was assessed by using two structural analogues of PM. In addition, the effects of pH, initial PM concentration, and photocatalyst dosage on the removal efficiency of PM were investigated [42]. The structure and properties of the prepared nanocomposite were characterized by FE-SEM, EDX, TEM, XRD, XPS, FT-IR, UV–vis DRS, and BET.

O-Phenylenediamine (OPDA), Titanium (IV) chloride, Ammonium persulfate (APS), Ammoniumhydroxide (25–30%), Ammonium (II) sulfate, Rhodamin B, Sodium carbonate, Ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA), and p-Benzoquinone (BQ) were obtained from Merck company. 2,4-Dinitroaniline (DNA) and 2-Nitro-p-Anisidine (NA) were purchased from Fluka. Hydrochloric acid, Ethanol, and Methanol were obtained from the Ameretat company of Iran. Ultrapure water was made by a Millipore water purification system. Pendimethalin (PM) (technical grade 95%) was obtained from Shanghai Cheng Bridge International Trade Co.

The FE-SEM and EDX analyses were done by a FE-SEM device (MIRA3 TESCAN). The crystalline structure of the nanocomposites was investigated by means of a STOE XD-3A X-ray diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation at λ = 1.54056 A° and operated at 40 kV and 40 mA. A UV-S600 spectrophotometer (Analytik Jena) was used for the UV–vis characterization. The zeta potential and size distribution of the samples were determined by the dynamic light scattering method using a Nano-ZS ZEN3600 device from Malvern Instruments. The TEM analyses were carried out with a Philips CM300, 200 KV. The diffuse reflectance UV–vis spectra of the samples were recorded by an Ava Spec-2048TEC spectrophotometer. The FT-IR analyses were done by a BOMEM MB-series FT-IR spectrometer using KBr pellets. An X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Bestec) equipped with an AlKα (1486.6 eV) X-ray source was used at a pressure of 10−10 mbar to determine the surface chemical composition of the nanocomposites. CasaXPS software was used for XPS data analysis. The textural properties of the samples were determined using the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) adsorption-desorption of N2 at −196 °C by means of a Belsorp mini II (the degassing temperature was 120 °C for 12 h). A Metrohm digital pH-meter 827 was employed for measuring the pH of the solutions. The fluorescence spectra were obtained using an LS45 fluorescence spectrometer (PerkinElmer) equipped with a xenon lamp as the light source. The samples were illuminated with 168 Bridgelux LED lamps (3.2 V, 1 W, 350 mA, λ > 390 nm) in a circular manner.

At first, 0.4 g of OPDA was dissolved in 20 mL ultrapure water using an ultrasonic device for 5 min. Then, the solution was sealed into a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 18 h. A brownish yellow solution was obtained, indicating the formation of carbon nanodots. After cooling down to room temperature, the solution was filtered through a Whatman filter paper to remove the large and agglomerated particles. The solution was further dialyzed in the ultrapure water through a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cut-off of 3500 Da for 24 h. Finally, the purified CNDs solution was dried at 60 °C in a vacuum oven [43,44].

In the first step, the TiO2 nanoparticles were synthesized based on the report of a previous work [45]. In a typical experiment, 50 mL of a 1.5 M aqueous solution of ammonium sulfate was added to a 0.75 M titanium (IV) chloride solution and stirred for 5 min. The solution was refluxed at 75 °C for 90 min. After that, a solution of 2.5 M ammonium hydroxide was added dropwise under high-speed stirring until a neutral pH was achieved. The precipitated titanium hydroxide was filtered and washed with water and ethanol and dried at 60 °C. The TiO2 nanoparticles were obtained by calcination in air at 350 °C for 4 h and cooling down to room temperature.

The TiO2/CNDs nanocomposite was prepared by a simple and facile process at room temperature [25]. First, 0.5 g of TiO2 was dispersed in 2.4 mL ultrapure water and added to 2.6 mL CNDs solution (1 mg mL−1) followed by vigorous stirring for 1 h at room temperature. Subsequently, TiO2/CNDs nanocomposite was collected by drying at 65 °C for 90 min in a vacuum oven.

The polymerization and preparation steps of MIP are illustrated in Scheme 1 . Typically, 0.5 g of PM and 1 g of OPDA were dissolved into 32 mL methanol and 128 mL ultrapure water and stirred for 20 min to form a complex between the template (PM) and monomer (OPDA). The pH of the solution was adjusted to pH = 2 by the addition of 6 M HCl solution. Afterward, 0.5 g of TiO2/CNDs was added to the solution and the mixture was sonicated for 10 min. Then, 20 mL of a 0.5 M HCl solution containing 1.8 g ammonium persulfate was added dropwise to the suspension solution at 0–5 °C. The resulting mixture was allowed to react at 0–5 °C for 4 h. The final suspension was centrifuged and washed by Na2CO3 solution (0.2 g/L) 5 times. The TiO2/CNDs/MIP nanocomposite was obtained after being washed with a mixture of methanol and distilled water several times to remove the template molecules, oligomers, and unreacted monomers and dried at 60 °C in a vacuum oven [41,46]. Similarly, TiO2/CNDs/NIP nanocomposite (acting as the control group) was also synthesized when no template molecule (PM) was used in the polymerization process.

The photocatalytic performances of the prepared TiO2 nanoparticles and TiO2/CNDs, TiO2/CNDs/MIP, and TiO2/CNDs/NIP nanocomposites in the degradation of PM herbicide were evaluated under visible light irradiation. First, 10 mg of each photocatalyst was suspended in 100 mL of a 20 ppm PM solution (a mixture of methanol/water (2:3, v/v), pH = 4.5). Meanwhile, for a better comparison, 5 mg of TiO2 (used as the control) was added to 20 ppm of the PM solution (based on the amount of Ti in the MIP nanocomposite obtained from the EDX analysis [47]). Then, the mixture was sonicated for 1–2 min and stirred for 1 h in the dark at room temperature to achieve an adsorption-desorption equilibrium. The suspension was irradiated by using LED lamps (λ > 390 nm) as the visible light source (the emission spectrum of the utilized LED lamps is shown in Fig. S1). Under stirring, a small amount of the suspension (3 mL) was taken out at regular time intervals and centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 5 min. The concentrations of PM were measured by a UV–vis spectrophotometer at λ = 240 nm. The photocatalytic efficiency of PM degradation was calculated using the following equation:

10 mg of TiO2/CNDs/MIP or TiO2/CNDs/NIP nanocomposite was added to 70 mL of the PM solution (a mixture of methanol and water (2:3, v/v), pH = 4.5) with initial concentrations ranging from 5 to 20 ppm. The mixture was stirred for 90 min and centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 5 min. The PM concentration was measured with a UV–vis spectrophotometer using a standard curve. The adsorption capacity (Q) was determined by the following Eq. [48]:

The selectivity tests of the adsorption process were carried out using DNA and NA as analogue structures (the structures of PM, DNA, and NA are shown in Fig. S2). 10 mg of TiO2/CNDs/MIP or TiO2/CNDs/NIP nanocomposite was dispersed in each solution which contained 70 mL of 15 ppm analytes. Then, the mixtures were stirred at room temperature for 30 min, centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 5 min, and the final concentrations of DNA and NA were determined by a UV–vis spectrophotometer at λ = 345 nm and λ = 234 nm, respectively.

Moreover, to further evaluate the selectivity, separate photodegradation experiments of DNA and NA were carried out using the same procedure as mentioned above with TiO2, MIP and NIP catalysts (the pollutant concentration: 20 ppm, the mass of the photocatalyst: 10 mg). The selectivity factors (SF) were measured according to the following equations [47,49]:

In this equation, Q, I, and A are the quantum yield, the measured intensity of PL spectra, and the absorbance value at the excitation wavelength, respectively. The quantum yield of CNDs was calculated by calibrating them against Rhodamine B (subscripted as ‘R') in ethanol as a standard (quantum yield = 90%).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The PL and UV–vis of the CNDs

ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the crystal structure of the nanoparticles, carbon nanodots, and nanocomposites, XRD analysis was utilized. Fig. 3 displays the XRD patterns of the TiO2 nanoparticles, carbon nanodots, TiO2/CNDs, and TiO2/CNDs/MIP nanocomposites. In the XRD pattern for TiO2 nanoparticles peaks at 2θ = 25.3, 37.83, 48.09, 54.96, 62.60, and 74.72 indicate the formation of anatase TiO2 (JCPDS No. 21–1272). The XRD pattern of CNDs with peaks at 2θ = 14.48, 22, and 26.92 is indexed to the lattice plane of carbon [52,53] and some impurity peaks are attributed to incomplete carbonization and residual groups from OPDA remaining on the surface of CNDs. Doping of CNDs on the surface of TiO2 nanoparticles had no obvious influence on the crystallinity and phase purity of the TiO2/CNDs due to the little content of CNDs. No apparent diffraction peaks were found for MIP, compared to TiO2/CNDs, which reveals that the molecularly imprinted polymer did not influence the lattice structure.

To determine the elemental compositions and bonding configuration, the XPS analysis was conducted. An XPS analysis of the elemental compositions of TiO2/CNDs/MIP nanocomposite has been presented in Fig. 4 . In the full survey spectrum (Fig. 4 (a)), the observed peaks can be attributed to the presence of Ti, C, N, and O elements. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of Ti 2p in Fig. 4 (b) shows two strong peaks of Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2 centered at 457.6 eV and 463.4 eV, respectively. The peak position of Ti 2p3/2 is related to the oxidation state of Ti4+ [54]. The sharp peaks appearing at 284.54 eV, 285.91 eV, and 287.35 eV in the spectrum of C1s (Fig. 4 (c)) are assigned to C-C/C=C, CN, and CO bonds, respectively, in TiO2/CNDs/MIP. Furthermore, the peak at 398.27 eV is attributed to the CN bond of the nanocomposite in the spectrum of N1s (Fig. 4 (d)) [25]. In addition, Ti-O-Ti and CO bonds were proved with peaks at 529 eV and 530.7 eV, respectively, in the spectrum of O1s (Fig. (4 (e)) [30].
Furthermore, Table 1 reports the measured XPS elemental compositions of the TiO2/CNDs/MIP nanocomposite.

To demonstrate the morphology of the nanoparticles and nanocomposites, the SEM images are presented in Fig. 5 . The spherical nanoparticles of TiO2 form a robust and dense network structure and have a particle size of about 15–20 nm (Fig. 5 (a)). When CNDs are attached to the surface of TiO2 nanoparticles, a porous and monodispersed morphology is observed (Fig. 5 (b)) and the particle size of TiO2/CNDs is about 20–25 nm. According to the particle size distribution of carbon nanodots (Fig. 2), the size of particles is mainly about 2 nm. Therefore, one can conclude that an aggregation has occurred among the particles of the carbon nanodots. The morphologies of TiO2/CNDs/MIP and TiO2/CNDs/NIP in Fig. 5 (c) and 5 (d) show homogeneous structures which have larger particle sizes (about 25–30 nm) than TiO2/CNDs because the thin polymer layers have been successfully coated on the surface of TiO2/CNDs. The EDX spectra of TiO2/CNDs and TiO2/CNDs/MIP are displayed in Fig. 6 and prove the presence of the expected elements in the nanocomposites and the comparison of the weight percent of the elements indicates the existence of poly o-phenylenediamine in the TiO2/CNDs/MIP nanocomposite.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image in Fig. 7 (a) reveals that TiO2/CNDs nanocomposite is composed of small particles of carbon nanodots with the average diameter of 5–8 nm which are uniformly distributed in close contact with the TiO2 nanoparticles. In addition, the aggregation of the carbon nanodots is confirmed by TEM image of TiO2/CNDs nanocomposite. Fig. 7 (b) shows the TEM image of TiO2/CNDs/MIP and demonstrates that the surface of TiO2/CNDs nanocomposite has become unclear with the thin layer of polymer. This indicates that the polymer layer has been constructed. In addition, the TEM image of TiO2/CNDs/NIP is shown in Fig. 7 (c) and further illustrates the formation of the polymer layer.

To identify the functional groups existing in the synthesized nanoparticles and nanocomposites, the FT-IR spectroscopy was employed. According to the analysis of FT-IR spectroscopy (Fig. 8 ), a double peak at 3384 cm−1 and 3363 cm−1 proves the existence of amino functional groups and another double broad peak at 3180 cm−1 and 3282 cm−1 indicates the existence of –OH groups on the surfaces of the CNDs [43]. Furthermore, some peaks at 1630–1770 cm−1, 1591–1458 cm−1, 1275 cm−1, 1155 cm−1, and 750 cm−1 demonstrate that CNDs include such groups as CO, CC, C-N=, and –CH bonds and the existence of CO and C-O-C groups is confirmed by peaks at 1055–1032 cm−1 and 928 cm−1 [43,52] which shows that a lot of unsaturated carbon bonds were formed during the carbonization process. In the spectrum of the TiO2 nanoparticle, the peak at 3440 cm−1 is related to the stretching vibration of OH and the peak at 1612 cm−1 is related to the bending vibration of OH (Ti-O-H), and the broad peak at 694 cm−1 is attributed to the TiO stretching band which is the characteristic peak of TiO2 [55]. In the FT-IR spectrum of the TiO2/CNDs, the major differences observed with respect to the TiO2 nanoparticles are related to the enhanced presence of the vibrational band at 3109 cm−1 corresponding to the OH group as well as the peaks at 1628 cm−1 and 1713 cm−1 which, depending on the CNDs, are ascribed to CC and CO stretching vibrations, respectively [25]. Additionally, the absorption bands detected at 1250 cm−1, 1134 cm−1, and 980 cm−1 compared to those of the uncoated TiO2 demonstrate an effective interaction with the support through the combination of Ti-O-C and Ti-O-Ti vibrational bands. The other peaks at 1543 cm−1 and 1396 cm−1 prove the presence of amide groups through the stretching vibrations of NH and CN, respectively [25]. After polymerization, the main characteristic absorption bands of the poly OPDA and PM can be found in the spectrum of TiO2/CNDs/Polymer. The stretching bands of –NH2 emerge at 3375 cm−1 and 3317 cm−1, and the strong band at 1527 cm−1 demonstrates the bending vibration of the –NH bond. The stretching mode of the = C-H bond on the benzene ring appears at 3128, and the peaks at 1321 cm−1 and 1240 cm−1 are related to the = C-N bond on the aromatic rings in the PM and poly-OPDA [41]. The existence of NO2 and aliphatic groups in the PM molecule is confirmed by peaks at 1543 cm−1, 1371 cm−1, and 2964 cm−1, respectively. Besides, the CC stretching vibrations of the benzenoid and quinoid rings of poly OPDA are assigned to the absorption bands at 1560 cm−1 and 1458 cm−1 [56]. The FT-IR spectra of pure poly OPDA and PM are shown in Fig. S5 as the reference spectra. Furthermore, the FT-IR analysis of TiO2/CNDs/MIP is almost the same as that of NIP, indicating that the template molecules of PM have been successfully removed. The related peaks of the benzenoid and quinoid rings of the polymer can also be observed. Polyaniline has a band at about 1100 cm−1 corresponding to the delocalization of electrons in a polymer backbone. The peaks at 1151 cm−1, 1117 cm−1, and 1147 cm−1 confirm the presence of a polyaniline-like structure in the MIP, NIP, and polymer spectra, respectively [57].

The UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra of the TiO2 nanoparticles and TiO2/CNDs, TiO2/CNDs/MIP, and TiO2/CNDs/NIP nanocomposites were investigated to compare their light absorption properties and the results are shown in Fig. 9 . The TiO2/CNDs, TiO2/CNDs/MIP, and TiO2/CNDs/NIP nanocomposites exhibit a high absorption in both the UV and visible regions compared with TiO2 nanoparticle which shows a light response only in the UV region. This indicates that CNDs broaden the light absorption range of the photocatalyst by creating conjugated and hybridized energy levels [25]. After TiO2/CNDs is coated with polymer, the absorption boundary is redshifted and the absorption intensity is further enhanced due to the creation of an organic polymer with a polyaniline-like structure that requires a lower amount of energy to produce electrons and holes. As a result, the photocatalytic activity is improved.

The band gap energies (Eg) of the TiO2 nanoparticles and TiO2/CNDs, TiO2/CNDs/MIP, and TiO2/CNDs/NIP nanocomposites are estimated by Tauc's plot using the following relation [58];
(8)
α =
C
hѵ − Eg
hѵ
where α, C, hѵ, and Eg are the absorption coefficient, a constant, the photon energy, and the band.

gap. The band gap energies of the nanocomposites were calculated by extrapolating the linear region of (αhν)2 versus the hѵ axis. As is shown in Fig. 10 , the Eg values of TiO2/CNDs, TiO2/CNDs/NIP, and TiO2/CNDs/MIP nanocomposites are 2 eV, 1.9 eV, and about 1.6 eV, respectively. The results could be attributed to the doping of carbon nanodots which could decrease the band gap energy of the TiO2/CNDs photocatalyst compared with that of TiO2 nanoparticles (about 3.2 eV) and improve the number of photogenerated electrons and holes [51]. Besides, the polymerization process which places some organic parts on the surface of TiO2/CNDs could enhance the absorbance of light. In addition, as a photosensitizer, o-phenylendiamine can have a synergic effect through charge separation which, as a result, decreases the Eg of TiO2/CNDs/NIP and TiO2/CNDs/MIP nanocomposites [46].

The textural characterizations of TiO2/CNDs/polymer, TiO2/CNDs/MIP, and TiO2/CNDs/NIP nanocomposites were carried out through N2 adsorption-desorption measurements. Table 2 reports the specific surface area (SBET), the mean pore diameter (dp), and the total pore volume (Vp) of the samples. The specific surface area of TiO2/CNDs/MIP was larger than those of TiO2/CNDs/polymer and TiO2/CNDs/NIP which is due to the existence of specific recognition cavities after removing the template molecules from the polymer layer. Before the extraction of the template molecules (TiO2/CNDs/polymer), the polymer layer had a smaller surface area than MIP. This difference in surface area could be related to the remove of PM molecules which increases the porosity of the surface area of MIP. Moreover, the mean pore diameter and the total pore volume of TiO2/CNDs/MIP increased compared to those of TiO2/CNDs/polymer and TiO2/CNDs/NIP, as expected from the imprinted materials.

Adsorption kinetics were studied to investigate the adsorption process. The effect of different initial concentrations on the adsorption of PM by TiO2/CNDs/MIP and TiO2/CNDs/NIP nanocomposites is shown in Fig. S6. According to Fig. S6, the Q values of TiO2/CNDs/MIP are 5.81 mg/g, 20.4 mg/g, 86.1 mg/g, and 84 mg/g for C = 5 ppm, 10 ppm, 15 ppm, and 20 ppm, respectively. Both MIP and NIP nanocomposites have the maximum adsorption capacity toward 15 ppm of the PM solution in 30 min. In Fig. 11 , the adsorption rates of TiO2/CNDs/MIP and TiO2/CNDs/NIP nanocomposites toward PM in 90 min are illustrated. The adsorption rate of MIP increased sharply in the first 30 min and its adsorption capacity (Q) reached 86.1 mg/g. Afterward, the amount of adsorption had no obvious change in the next 30 min. The adsorption equilibrium was established from 60 to 90 min. In addition, the adsorption rate of TiO2/CNDs/NIP (Q was 9.1 mg/g in 30 min) was much lower than that of TiO2/CNDs/MIP. Therefore, the value of the imprinting factor was considerable (9.46). The results demonstrated that the PM molecules reached the surface imprinting cavities of the MIP layer easily indicating that TiO2/CNDs/MIP nanocomposite had the property of good mass transport. The adsorption equilibrium was accomplished when the recognition sites were filled up and released a number of PM molecules, as a result, the rate of adsorption gradually decreased.
The adsorption rate of the prepared MIP has been compared with those of similar studies in the literature in Table 3 .

In order to investigate the adsorption process mechanism of the MIP nanocomposite, the following linear forms of the pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order kinetic models were applied to fit the experimental data.

where Qe and Qt are the adsorption capacities (mg/g) at equilibrium and time (t), respectively. k1 (min−1) and k2 (g mg−1 min−1) are the rate constants of the pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order kinetic models, respectively [61]. The values of k1 and k2 were calculated from the plots of log (Qe – Qt) versus t and (t/Qt) versus t, respectively. The determination coefficient (R2) value of the pseudo-second order (R2 > 0.98) was much higher than that of the pseudo-first order. Besides, the calculated Qe value for the pseudo-second order was close to the experimental one (Table 4 ). Therefore, the pseudo-second-order kinetic model was more appropriate to demonstrate the adsorption behavior of PM on the MIP nanocomposite. The curve of the pseudo-second-order adsorption kinetics of MIP is shown in Fig. S7.

The Langmuir and Freundlich isotherm models are widely employed to characterize the adsorption of solutes from aqueous solutions. The Langmuir isotherm explains that all binding sites are equivalent, resulting in the formation of a monolayer of the adsorbed molecules onto a surface (the saturation of the adsorbent). The Freundlich isotherm is applied for adsorption onto heterogeneous surfaces whose binding sites have varying adsorption affinities. As a result, the adsorbent will not be saturated [62]. The linear forms of the Langmuir and Freundlich isotherms can be described by the following Eqs. [63]:

Langmuir model

where Ce (mg/L) is the concentration of PM at equilibrium, Qe (mg/g) is the corresponding adsorption capacity, and Qm (mg/g) is a constant related to adsorption capacity. KL (L/mg) is the Langmuir constant which correlates with the affinity of the binding sites. KF and n are the Freundlich constants that represent the adsorption capacity and intensity, respectively [61]. Hence, KL and Qm can be obtained by plotting Ce/Qe against Ce and Freundlich constants can be determined by plotting log Qe versus log Ce. The curves of the Langmuir and Freundlich adsorption isotherms for TiO2/CNDs/MIP are depicted in Fig. S8 (a) and Fig. S8 (b), respectively. The correlation coefficient (R2) of the Langmuir isotherm suggests that this model can generate a good fit to the experimental data (Table 5 ).

In order to investigate the adsorption selectivity of TiO2/CNDs/MIP and TiO2/CNDs/NIP nanocomposites toward pendimethalin, its structural analogues (DNA and NA) were chosen and the results are shown in Fig. 12 . The adsorption amounts of TiO2/CNDs/MIP toward PM, DNA, and NA were higher than those of TiO2/CNDs/NIP. The larger selectivities of TiO2/CNDs/MIP are attributed to the interactions between the imprinted cavities and the functional groups of molecules. The excellent adsorption capacity of TiO2/CNDs/MIP toward PM is related to the specific recognition cavities on its surface.

The photocatalytic activities of the TiO2 nanoparticles and TiO2/CNDs, TiO2/CNDs/MIP, and TiO2/CNDs/NIP nanocomposites were tested by the degradation of a solution containing PM, as a recalcitrant and toxic herbicide pollutant, under visible light irradiation. Fig. 13: (a) shows the photodegradation efficiency of PM for synthesized catalysts in different irradiation times. The photolysis was used as a control system and the experiments had no PM degradation in the absence of catalysts. The photocatalytic activity of the TiO2 nanoparticles was investigated as the second control system. The imprinted photocatalyst contains both the precursor (TiO2, in this work) and the matrix. In order to compare the photocatalytic activity of the TiO2 nanoparticles and the imprinted photocatalyst, a reliable comparison could be carried out if the percentage of the TiO2 nanoparticles in the imprinted photocatalyst is estimated [47]. Based on the EDX analysis in Fig. 6 (b), the percentage of Ti in the MIP nanocomposite was 28.6% which is equivalent to 47.7% of TiO2. In the photocatalytic activity measurement experiment, the mass of the MIP photocatalyst was 10 mg. Hence, 4.77 mg (~5 mg) of TiO2 was used as the control. The degradation efficiency of TiO2 nanoparticles (25.3%) was lower than that of TiO2/CNDs (which was 76%) due to the large band gap and the UV-light response of TiO2 nanoparticles. It is clear that the doping of CNDs on TiO2 nanoparticle surfaces enhanced the visible-light-driven photodegradation efficiency from 25.3% to 76%. The enhancement of the photocatalytic performance of TiO2/CNDs can be attributed to the upconversion process in which CNDs absorb visible light and then emit a shorter wavelength light (325 to 425 nm). As a result, TiO2 nanoparticles are excited with a wavelength shorter than 388 nm (Eg = 3.2 eV) to form electron/hole (e−/h+) pairs. Then, the electron/hole pairs react with the adsorbed oxidants/reducers (O2 and OH−) to produce super-oxide radical anions (O2
−) and OH radicals which subsequently cause the degradation of the PM. Remarkably, when CNDs are doped on the TiO2 surface, the correlative position of the band edge of CNDs permits the transfer of electrons from the surface of TiO2 which results in stabilization, charge carrier separation, and hindered recombination [51]. The photodegradation of PM by TiO2/CNDs/MIP demonstrated the maximum efficiency (about 95%). This excellent photocatalytic activity is attributed to the high light-absorption intensity in visible light area, the narrow band gap energy and strong adsorption of MIP toward the PM [60].

It is well known that the kinetics of light-driven degradation of organic contaminants match with a first-order reaction model, obeying the Langmuir-Hinshelwood model [64]. The kinetics of the photodegradation are determined by the following equation:
(13)
− Ln

C 0
= kt
where C0 and C are PM concentrations at t = 0 and the later time t, respectively, and k is the apparent rate reaction constant. Fig. 13(b) shows - Ln(C/C0) as a function of visible light irradiation time (up to 120 min) over TiO2 nanoparticles and TiO2/CNDs, TiO2/CNDs/MIP, and TiO2/CNDs/NIP nanocomposites. It can be seen that TiO2/CNDs/MIP exhibited the highest k value (0.0323 min−1) which was 13.45, 2.6, and 2.99 times higher than those of TiO2 (0.0024 min−1), TiO2/CNDs (0.0124 min−1), and TiO2/CNDs/NIP (0.0108 min−1), respectively. The linear relationship (> 95%) in the curves indicates that the potodegradation of PM had a pseudo-first-order kinetic. Table 6 compares the data obtained from the MIP photocatalyst in the current study with those of some recent studies on selective photodegradation of some pollutants.

Furthermore, to achieve the best photocatalytic performance, the optimization of some important experimental parameters such as the concentrations of the catalysts and PM as well as the pH of the solutions were investigated. As is shown in the supporting information, the highest photocatalyst performance was obtained with 100 ppm of the photocatalyst and 20 ppm of PM at pH = 4 under visible light irradiation in 120 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of PM concentration on the removal of PM using TiO2/CNDs/MIP
ce:section.ce:para:
The removal efficiency of PM was studied by changing the initial concentration of PM from 5 to 30 ppm. The data in Fig. S9 show that the photodegradation rate is greatly affected by the concentration of PM. With the increase of Co in the range of 5–20 ppm, a perfect degradation is realized in 120 min. However, for higher amounts of Co, the degradation efficiency decreased. This result can be attributed to the large number of PM molecules adsorbed on the photocatalyst surface blocking the active sites and decreasing the number of photogenerated reactive radicals (O2
− and OH) as the main force of degradation. In addition, the penetration of light becomes difficult and, consequently, the photons cannot contribute to the photocatalysis process very well [12].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of the solution pH on the removal of PM using TiO2/CNDs/MIP
ce:section.ce:para: Since most semiconductor oxides have an amphoteric characteristic, the solution pH is one of the most important factors which influence the surface charge of photocatalyst, ionization, and adsorption of the pollutant. Additionally, it changes the levels of conduction and valence bands in the photocatalyst structure [65]. The degradation efficiency of PM was evaluated by adjusting the pH of solutions in the range of 2 to 10 by adding KOH (0.01 M) or HCl (0.01 M) before the addition of photocatalyst. The results are illustrated in Fig. S10. The removal efficiency of PM increased remarkably from 63.8% to 94.4% with the increase of pH from 2 to 4, while it decreased from 94.4% to 51.4% with the increase of pH from 4 to 6. When pH was 8, the degradation rate of PM reached its minimum and the photocatalytic activity of TiO2/CNDs/MIP nanocomposite was 73.5% at pH =10. The zeta potential was used as a physical parameter to determine the electrical potential of the surface of TiO2/CNDs/MIP and the values of 1.36 mV, −1.23 mV, and − 0.989 mV were obtained after 3 measurements. It could be assumed that the surface charge was around zero while the related aqueous solution had pH = 4.5, as a result, pHpzc (point of zero charge) = 4.5. When pH is less than pHpzc, the surface is positively charged, conversely, when it is more than pHpzc, the surface is negatively charged. Meanwhile, pendimethalin is a secondary amine and has negative oxygen atoms in its NO2 groups. In addition, the pKa value of PM is 2.8 [66] and PM tends to exist in its anionic form at pH > 2.8. Therefore, N− and the negative oxygen atoms of PM can be attracted to the positive charge of the surface of MIP at pH < pHpzc [60]. This attraction increases the adsorption of PM on the surface of MIP, leading to a higher photodegradation efficiency. On the contrary, when pH > pHpzc, the anions of PM can be repulsed from the negative charge of the surface of MIP which decreases the photocatalytic activity. Moreover, under basic conditions, the OH radicals are more easily generated by oxidizing more hydroxide ions which results in an enhanced degradation at pH = 10 [13,67]. Therefore, pH = 4 was chosen as the optimum working pH considering the fact that the solution of PM in methanol and water had pH = 4.5 and there was no need to adjust the pH of the reaction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of catalyst concentration on the removal of PM using TiO2/CNDs/MIP
ce:section.ce:para: One of the other parameters that changes the photocatalytic performance is the catalyst dose. Therefore, the photocatalytic degradation of PM was investigated in the catalyst concentration range of 50–200 ppm. The obtained data from Fig. S11 reveal that the maximum photodegradation activity was achieved in 100 ppm of catalyst. This can be due to the fact that a high number of nanocomposites decreased light penetration.

The photocatalytic activities of TiO2 nanoparticles, TiO2/CNDs/NIP, and TiO2/CNDs/MIP nanocomposites for the degradation of DNA and NA under visible light are shown in Fig. 14 . The TiO2 nanoparticles exhibited no significant difference in the photodegradation of DNA, NA, and PM (17.78%, 17.2%, and 25.3%, respectively). However, the photocatalytic efficiency of TiO2/CNDs/MIP for the degradation of PM was much higher than those of its structural analogues (PM: 94.4%, DNA: 26.7%, and NA: 20.7%). This is in good agreement with the intrinsic molecular specific recognition and the selectivity adsorption of MIP toward the template molecules. It was found that the photodegradation activities of TiO2/CNDs/NIP for DNA and NA (17.5% and 11.7%, respectively) were lower than that of PM (69.73%). Therefore, it seems that the experimental parameters of the photodegradation of DNA and NA by the TiO2/CNDs/NIP nanocomposite need to be optimized to achieve a better photodegradation efficiency. The selectivity factors of MIP and NIP toward PM and its structural analogues were calculated and the results are presented in Table 7 . In addition, the values of the selectivity factors based on TiO2 were calculated as 5.3 for DNA and 5.5 for NA.

To evaluate the stability and reusability of the TiO2/CNDs/MIP nanocomposite, a recycling test was performed. The used catalysts were extracted by methanol to remove the absorbed PM for the recycling experiment. Fig. 15 demonstrates slight decreases in the photocatalyst efficiency of MIP after it was repeatedly used and recycled five times in the photodegradation of PM, suggesting the good reproducibility and retention of MIP nanocomposite.

To understand the role of reactive oxygen species in the degradation of PM, the effects of several charge carrier scavengers such as EDTA, methanol, and BQ as hole, OH radical, and O2
− radical scavenger, respectively, with the concentrations of 1 mM were evaluated to determine the reaction mechanism [68]. As depicted in Fig. 16 , the photocatalytic degradation of PM was remarkably reduced by the addition of BQ (45.9%), compared to the effects of EDTA and methanol whose photodegradation efficiencies were 87.16% and 80.2%, respectively. Thus, one can conclude that the O2
− radicals have a more active role than holes and OH radicals in the mechanism.
The possible mechanism of TiO2/CNDs/MIP photocatalyst under visible light irradiation has been proposed in Scheme 2 .

When TiO2/CNDs/MIP nanocomposite are irradiated under visible light, the electrons of the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) can be excited to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of polymer while the holes are retained in the HOMO of the polymer. Simultaneously, the upconversion process of doped carbon nanodots on TiO2 surfaces occurs and the electrons are excited from the valence band (VB) of TiO2 to its conduction band (CB) and electron/hole (e−/h+) pairs are formed. According to the above scavenging experiment and the important role of the O2
− radicals, it can be concluded that the electrons in the LUMO of the polymer can migrate to the VB of TiO2 to recombine with the holes. Thus, the holes in the HOMO of the polymer reduce H2O into the reactive species OH radical, whereas the electrons in the CB of TiO2 can react by such electron acceptors as O2 adsorbed on the TiO2 nanoparticles or dissolved in water to produce O2
− radicals. Moreover, pendimethalin can directly be reduced by holes, becoming CO2 and H2O [59]. Finally, the active species can degrade the adsorbed PM onto the molecularly imprinted layer to mineral products.

A novel, smooth, and spherical monodispersed TiO2/CNDs/MIP nanocomposite was fabricated by surface molecularly imprinted technique using PM herbicide as the template molecule and OPDA as the functional monomer. TiO2/CNDs/MIP nanocomposite exhibited a higher adsorption capacity for the PM target than TiO2/CNDs/NIP nanocomposite which could be ascribed to the imprinted cavities and the specific recognition sites on the surface of MIP. The DRS results showed the redshifted absorption to the visible regions and the decrease of the band gap energy of MIP nanocomposite. TiO2/CNDs/MIP nanocomposite demonstrated the enhancement of photocatalytic activity and selectivity for the degradation of the PM target as compared to TiO2 nanoparticles, TiO2/CNDs, and TiO2/CNDs/NIP nanocomposites under visible light. The results could be attributed to the creation of conjugated and hybridized energy levels of CNDs with TiO2 and the formation of an imprinted polymer with a polyaniline-like structure that required a lower amount of energy to produce electrons and holes. A number of charge carrier scavengers were used in order to understand the main species involved in the photodegradation of pendimethalin. The O2
− radicals were found to be the main species responsible for photodegradation over the MIP catalyst. Furthermore, the TiO2/CNDs/MIP photocatalyst exhibited excellent reproducibility and stability. Finally, it is proposed that the new MIP nanocomposite can be a convenient photocatalyst for the effective removal of pollutants from the environment.
